encounters.chief_complaint: Congestion, runny nose, mild cough for 3 days
encounters.hpi: 10-year-old male presents with 3-day history of nasal congestion, clear rhinorrhea, and mild dry cough. Low-grade fever to 100.2F yesterday. Decreased appetite but drinking fluids well. No ear pain, sore throat, or difficulty breathing. Parents gave ibuprofen for fever with good response. History of asthma - no increase in rescue inhaler use. Type 1 diabetes - blood sugars slightly elevated but no ketones.

physical_exam.general: Well-appearing child, mild nasal congestion, no respiratory distress
physical_exam.heent: Erythematous nasal mucosa with clear discharge. TMs clear bilaterally. Pharynx mildly erythematous without exudate. No tonsillar enlargement.
physical_exam.neck: Supple, small anterior cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally
physical_exam.cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmur
physical_exam.respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, no crackles, good air movement

assessment.clinical_notes: Typical viral URI presentation. No bacterial superinfection.

plan.description: Continue ibuprofen PRN for fever/discomfort
Saline nasal spray as needed for congestion
Increase fluid intake, rest
Monitor blood sugars more frequently during illness
Continue current asthma and diabetes medications
Return if fever persists >5 days, develops ear pain, difficulty breathing, worsening cough, or blood sugar concerns

encounters.follow_up_instructions: Return if symptoms worsen or persist beyond 7-10 days

encounters.chief_complaint: Worsening cough, new fever after cold
encounters.hpi:
Patient was seen 9 days ago for upper respiratory infection symptoms. Initially improved over first 4-5 days, but over past 2-3 days developed worsening cough that is now productive. New fever to 102.4F starting yesterday. Decreased activity level, not wanting to eat. Some increased work of breathing noted by parents. Blood sugars running higher than usual despite increased insulin. Increased albuterol use to every 4 hours for cough/wheeze. 

Prior encounter ID: 7a134448

physical_exam.general: Ill-appearing child, appears fatigued, mild tachypnea at rest
physical_exam.heent: Nasal congestion improved. TMs clear. Pharynx mildly erythematous.
physical_exam.respiratory: Decreased breath sounds at right base. Crackles (rales) on right lower lobe. Mild expiratory wheezing diffusely. Subcostal retractions present.

assessment.clinical_notes: Secondary bacterial pneumonia following viral URI. Focal findings on exam, clinical syndrome consistent with CAP.

code.display: Mild intermittent asthma with acute exacerbation

plan.description: Amoxicillin 90mg/kg/day divided BID x 5 days (1372mg BID)
Continue albuterol nebulizer Q4H while awake x 48-72 hours, then transition back to MDI PRN
Prednisone 1mg/kg daily x 5 days for asthma exacerbation
Chest X-ray ordered to confirm pneumonia
Increase insulin by 10-20% during illness with steroid use
Check blood sugars every 3-4 hours, check ketones if BG >300
Push fluids, rest
Recheck in 48-72 hours or sooner if worsening
Return immediately or go to ED if: increasing work of breathing, SpO2 <92%, unable to take oral medications, persistent vomiting, or lethargy

prescriptions.instructions: Take with food. Complete entire course (5 days).
Take in the morning with breakfast. Will affect blood sugars. 5 day course.

encounters.narrative_note:
ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

10-year-old male with history of Type 1 Diabetes and asthma presents with worsening
respiratory symptoms 9 days after initial URI. Clinical presentation concerning for
secondary bacterial pneumonia with mild asthma exacerbation.

CLINICAL REASONING:
History of viral URI followed by initial improvement, then return of fever with productive
cough and focal lung findings (decreased breath sounds and crackles at right base) is
classic presentation for secondary bacterial pneumonia. The "double sickening" pattern -
initial improvement followed by worsening - is highly suggestive of bacterial superinfection.

Given concurrent asthma history with current wheezing and increased work of breathing,
treating concurrent asthma exacerbation with systemic steroids. Patient's diabetes
management will need close attention given illness and steroid use.

Signed: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD, FAAP
encounters.follow_up_instructions: Recheck in 48-72 hours. Return immediately if difficulty breathing worsens.

encounters.hpi:
10-year-old male with Type 1 Diabetes (diagnosed age 4) and asthma (diagnosed age 2) presents for combined chronic disease management follow-up. 

DIABETES: Using insulin pump (Omnipod), doing well with current settings. Downloading pump data shows time in range 65%, slightly below goal of 70%. Some afternoon lows noted, may need basal adjustment. Using Dexcom G7 CGM, calibrating regularly. Last HbA1c was 7.4% (goal <7.5%). No episodes of DKA or severe hypoglycemia. Sees endocrinology every 3 months.

ASTHMA: Well-controlled on current regimen. Using fluticasone 44mcg 2 puffs BID and albuterol PRN. Rescue inhaler use 1-2x per week, usually with exercise. Recent cold 2 months ago triggered mild exacerbation treated with oral steroids. No ED visits or hospitalizations this year. Peak flows at baseline.

physical_exam.general: Well-appearing, well-nourished child in no distress
physical_exam.heent: Normocephalic, PERRLA, TMs clear, oropharynx clear
physical_exam.respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, good air exchange
physical_exam.abdomen: Soft, NT/ND. Insulin pump site left abdomen - no erythema. CGM site right arm - healthy appearing.
physical_exam.skin: Multiple prior pump/CGM sites examined, no lipohypertrophy. No rashes.
physical_exam.neurological: Alert, appropriate, no focal deficits

assessment.diagnosis: Type 1 Diabetes mellitus - stable, fair control
assessment.clinical_notes: HbA1c 7.4%, time in range 65%. Some afternoon hypoglycemia.
Minimal rescue inhaler use, no nocturnal symptoms, no activity limitation.

plan.description: Continue current insulin pump settings; decrease afternoon basal by 0.05 units/hr from 2-5pm to address lows

encounters.chief_complaint: Laceration to forearm from bicycle fall
encounters.hpi:
10-year-old male presents after falling off bicycle approximately 1 hour ago. Was riding on sidewalk, hit a crack, and fell onto concrete. Landed on outstretched right arm. Sustained laceration to right forearm. No loss of consciousness, no head strike, was wearing helmet. Denies numbness/tingling in hand or fingers. Moving all digits well. Bleeding controlled with pressure by parents. 

PMH: Type 1 Diabetes, Asthma. No bleeding disorders. Immunizations up to date including tetanus (DTaP at 4 years, Tdap at age 10).

physical_exam.general: Alert, tearful but consolable, holding right arm
physical_exam.musculoskeletal: RIGHT FOREARM: 4cm linear laceration on volar aspect of mid-forearm. Wound edges clean, no debris visible. Bleeding controlled. Depth appears through dermis, subcutaneous tissue visible, no muscle or tendon involvement. Full ROM of wrist and fingers. Sensation intact to light touch in median, ulnar, radial distributions. Radial pulse 2+, capillary refill <2 seconds.
physical_exam.skin: Laceration as described. Multiple abrasions to right palm and knee, superficial. No other injuries noted.

code.display: Laceration without foreign body of unspecified forearm, initial encounter

assessment.clinical_notes: 4cm linear laceration, clean edges, requires suture repair

code.display: Abrasion of other part of head, initial encounter
Pedal cyclist injured in noncollision transport accident

plan.description: Wound irrigation with normal saline
Laceration repair with 4-0 nylon sutures, 8 sutures placed
Lidocaine 1% with epinephrine for local anesthesia
Ibuprofen 400mg PO for pain (given in office)
Ibuprofen 400mg PO Q6H PRN pain x 3 days
Wound care instructions provided: Keep dry x 24 hours, then may shower. No soaking or swimming.
Signs of infection to watch for: increasing redness, warmth, swelling, drainage, fever
Tetanus up to date - no booster needed
Return in 10-12 days for suture removal
Monitor blood sugars - stress can cause elevation

prescriptions.instructions: Take with food. Do not exceed 4 doses per day.

encounters.narrative_note:
PROCEDURE NOTE:

Informed consent obtained from parent. Procedure, risks, benefits, and alternatives discussed.

The wound was examined and found to be clean with well-approximated edges.
Wound irrigated with 200mL normal saline. No foreign bodies identified.

Local anesthesia achieved with 3mL of 1% lidocaine with epinephrine via wound edge infiltration.
Adequate anesthesia confirmed.

Wound closed with 8 simple interrupted sutures using 4-0 nylon.
Good approximation achieved. Hemostasis confirmed.

Sterile dressing applied.

Patient tolerated procedure well. Vital signs stable throughout.
Discharge instructions reviewed with parent.

Signed: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD, FAAP
encounters.follow_up_instructions: Return in 10-12 days for suture removal. Return sooner if signs of infection develop.

physical_exam.general: Well-appearing, well-nourished, in no acute distress
physical_exam.heent: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, reactive. TMs clear bilaterally. Oropharynx clear.
physical_exam.cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmur
physical_exam.respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi
physical_exam.musculoskeletal: Normal tone and strength, moves all extremities well
physical_exam.neurological: Alert, appropriate for age, normal tone

plan.description: Anticipatory guidance provided

patient_messages.subject: Medication refill needed
patient_messages.message_body: Hi, we need a refill on the regular medication. The pharmacy is Walmart Pharmacy. Thank you.
patient_messages.reply_body: Hi Camila, the refill for the regular medication has been sent to your pharmacy. Please allow 24-48 hours for processing.

patient_messages.subject: Question about symptoms
patient_messages.message_body: Hi, Owen has been having trouble sleeping for the past few days. Should we come in or is this something we can manage at home?
patient_messages.reply_body: Thank you for reaching out. Based on what you've described, here are some recommendations: use a cool mist humidifier. If symptoms worsen or you have concerns, please call the office or go to urgent care.

patient_messages.subject: Need to schedule appointment
patient_messages.message_body: Hello, we need to schedule an appointment for Owen. ongoing symptoms. What times do you have available this week?
patient_messages.reply_body: We have availability on Thursday at 2:30 PM. Please call the office at (555) 123-4567 to confirm, or reply to this message if that time works.

patient_messages.subject: Side effects question
patient_messages.message_body: We started Flovent last week and noticed some stomach upset. Is this normal? Should we continue?
patient_messages.reply_body: The symptoms you're describing can be a common side effect. Continue the medication unless symptoms worsen significantly. If you notice high fever over 104F, please call us right away.

patient_messages.subject: Question about symptoms
patient_messages.message_body: Hi, Owen has been having a rash for the past few days. Should we come in or is this something we can manage at home?
patient_messages.reply_body: Thank you for reaching out. Based on what you've described, here are some recommendations: ensure adequate hydration. If symptoms worsen or you have concerns, please call the office or go to urgent care.

patient_messages.subject: Question about lab results
patient_messages.message_body: Hi, I saw that Owen's lab results are in the portal. Can someone explain what they mean? I'm not sure if they're normal.
patient_messages.reply_body: I've reviewed the results and everything looks normal/within expected ranges. No action needed at this time. Let us know if you have any other questions.

patient_messages.subject: Prescription refill request
patient_messages.message_body: Hello, Owen is running low on albuterol inhaler. Can you please send a refill to the pharmacy? Thanks!
patient_messages.reply_body: Refill has been submitted. If you don't receive it within 2 business days, please call the pharmacy directly.

patient_messages.subject: Quick question - hoping to avoid visit
patient_messages.message_body: Hi, Owen has a headache. I'm trying to figure out if we need to come in or if there's something we can try at home first?
patient_messages.reply_body: Based on what you've described, you can try cool mist humidifier for the next 24-48 hours. If difficulty breathing, please come in for an evaluation.

issues.message: 'type 1 diabetes' has 27 follow-up(s) but no monitoring labs
issues.suggested_fix: Add hemoglobin a1c labs to chronic follow-up encounters
